entries.english: Wherever you see the grain, beware, lest the trap confine your wings and pinions,

entries.farsi: ز انكه مرغى كاو بترك دانه كرد / دانه از صحراى بى‏تزوير خورد
entries.english: Because the bird that takes leave of the grain, eats grain from the spacious field that is without imposture.

entries.farsi: هم بدان قانع شد و از دام جست / ‏هيچ دامى پر و بالش را نبست
entries.english: With that it is contented, and escapes the trap: no trap confines its wings and pinions.

sections.title_fa: وخامت كار آن مرغ كه ترك حزم كرد از حرص و هوا
sections.title_en: The harmfulness of the action of the bird that abandons prudence from greed and vain desire

entries.farsi: باز مرغى فوق ديوارى نشست / ‏ديده سوى دانه‏ى دامى ببست
entries.english: Again, a bird settles on a wall and fastens its eyes upon the grain in a trap.

entries.farsi: يك نظر او سوى صحرا مى‏كند / يك نظر حرصش به دانه مى‏كشد
entries.english: Now it looks towards the open country, now its greed leads it to look at the grain.

entries.farsi: اين نظر با آن نظر چاليش كرد / ناگهانى از خرد خاليش كرد
entries.english: This look struggles with that look and suddenly makes it empty of wisdom.

entries.farsi: باز مرغى كان تردد را گذاشت / ‏ز آن نظر بر كند و بر صحرا گماشت
entries.english: Again, a bird that has abandoned that vacillation turns its gaze away from that and fixes it upon the open fields.

entries.farsi: شاد پر و بال او بخا له / تا امام جمله آزادان شد او
entries.english: Glad are its wings and pinions: how goodly it is, since it has become the leader of all the free.

entries.farsi: هر كه او را مقتدا سازد برست / ‏در مقام امن و آزادى نشست
entries.english: Everyone who makes it his model is saved and sits in the abode of security and freedom,

entries.farsi: ز انكه شاه حازمان آمد دلش / ‏تا گلستان و چمن شد منزلش
entries.english: Because his heart has become the king of the prudent, so that the rose garden has become his dwelling-place

entries.farsi: حزم از او راضى و او راضى ز حزم / ‏اين چنين كن گر كنى تدبير و عزم
entries.english: Prudence is pleased with him, and he pleased with Prudence: do likewise, if you would act with foresight and resolution.

entries.farsi: بارها در دام حرص افتاده‏اى / ‏حلق خود را در بريدن داده‏اى
entries.english: Many times have you fallen into the snare of greed and given up your throat to be cut;

entries.farsi: بازت آن تواب لطف آزاد كرد / توبه پذرفت و شما را شاد كرد
entries.english: Again He that graciously disposes to repentance has set you free and accepted your repentance and made you glad.

entries.farsi: گفت إن عدتم كذا عدنا كذا / نحن زوجنا الفعال بالجزا
entries.english: He has said, “If you return thus, We will return thus: We have wedded the actions to the retribution.

entries.farsi: جفت كرديم اين عمل را با اثر / چون رسد جفتى رسد جفتى دگر
entries.english: When I bring one mate to Myself, the other mate inevitably comes running.

entries.farsi: چون كه جفتى را بر خود آورم / ‏آيد آن جفتش دوانه الجرم
entries.english: We have mated this action with the effect: when one mate arrives, another mate arrives.”

entries.farsi: چون ربايد غارتى از جفت شوى / ‏جفت مى‏آيد پس او شوى جوى
entries.english: When a raider carries off the husband from the wife, the wife comes after him, seeking her husband.

entries.farsi: بار ديگر سوى اين دام آمديد / خاك اندر ديده‏ى توبه زديد
entries.english: Once more you have come towards this snare and have thrown dust in the eyes of repentance.

entries.farsi: بازت آن تواب بگشاد آن گره / ‏گفت هين بگريز روى اين سو منه
entries.english: Again that Forgiving One has loosed that knot for you and has said, “Beware! Flee! Set not your face in this direction!”

entries.farsi: باز چون پروانه‏ى نسيان رسيد / جانتان را جانب آتش كشيد
entries.english: Again, when the mandate of forgetfulness arrived, it drew your soul towards the Fire.

entries.farsi: كم كن اى پروانه نسيان و شكى / ‏در پر سوزيده بنگر تو يكى
entries.english: O you moth, do not show any forgetfulness and doubt: look once at your burnt wing!

entries.farsi: چون رهيدى شكر آن باشد كه هيچ / ‏سوى آن دانه ندارى پيچ پيچ
entries.english: Since you are saved, the thanksgiving is this, that you should have no inclination towards that grain,

entries.farsi: تا ترا چون شكر گويى بخشد او / روزى بى‏دام و بى‏خوف عدو
entries.english: In order that, when you say thanks, He may bestow on you the daily bread that is without snare and without fear of the enemy.

entries.farsi: شكر آن نعمت كه‏تان آزاد كرد / نعمت حق را ببايد ياد كرد
entries.english: In thanksgiving for the bounty shown in setting you free, it behooves you to commemorate the bounty of God.

entries.farsi: چند اندر رنجها و در بال / گفتى از دامم رها ده اى خدا
entries.english: How often in sorrows and in tribulation have you cried, “O God, deliver me from the snare,

entries.farsi: تا چنين خدمت كنم احسان كنم / ‏خاك اندر ديده‏ى شيطان زنم
entries.english: That I may do such service and practice beneficence and throw dust in the eyes of the Devil!”

sections.title_fa: حكايت نذر كردن سگان هر زمستان كه اين تابستان چون بيايد خانه سازيم از بهر زمستان را
sections.title_en: Story of the vow made by the dogs every winter that when next summer comes they will build a house for the winter

entries.farsi: سگ زمستان جمع گردد استخوانش / ‏زخم سرما خرد گرداند چنانش
entries.english: In winter the bones of the dog are drawn together: the blows of the frost make him so small

entries.farsi: كاو بگويد كاين قدر تن كه منم / ‏خانه‏اى از سنگ بايد كردنم
entries.english: That he says, “Having such a little body, I must build a stone house.

entries.farsi: چون كه تابستان بيايد من به چنگ / ‏بهر سرما خانه‏اى سازم ز سنگ
entries.english: When summer comes, I will build with my claws a stone house against the cold.”

entries.farsi: چون كه تابستان بيايد از گشاد / استخوانها پهن گردد پوست شاد
entries.english: When summer comes, his bones expand from the relief, and his skin grows sleek,

entries.farsi: گويد او چون زفت بيند خويش را / در كدامين خانه گنجم اى كيا
entries.english: And, when he sees himself stout, he says, “In what house should I find room, O noble sir?”

entries.farsi: زفت گردد پا كشد در سايه‏اى / ‏كاهلى سيرى غرى خود رايه‏اى
entries.english: He grows stout and slinks into a shady place— a lazy, full-fed, cowardly, self-opinionated!

entries.farsi: گويدش دل خانه‏اى ساز اى عمو / گويد او در خانه كى گنجم بگو
entries.english: His heart says to him, “Build a house, O uncle!” He says, “How shall I find room in the house? Tell.”

entries.farsi: استخوان حرص تو در وقت درد / درهم آيد خرد گردد در نورد
entries.english: In the hour of pain the bones of your greed shrink together and diminish in compass,

entries.farsi: گويى از توبه بسازم خانه‏اى / ‏در زمستان باشدم استانه‏اى
entries.english: And you say penitently, “I will build a house: it will be a resting-place for me in winter”;

entries.farsi: چون بشد درد و شدت آن حرص زفت / همچو سگ سوداى خانه از تو رفت
entries.english: When the pain is gone and your greed has grown stout, the desire for the house departs from you, just as the dog.

entries.farsi: شكر نعمت خوشتر از نعمت بود / شكر باره كى سوى نعمت رود
entries.english: Thanksgiving for the bounty is sweeter than the bounty: how should he that is addicted to thanksgiving go towards the bounty?

entries.farsi: شكر جان نعمت و نعمت چو پوست / ‏ز انكه شكر آرد ترا تا كوى دوست
entries.english: Thanksgiving is the soul of the bounty, and the bounty is as the husk, because thanksgiving brings you to the abode of the Beloved.

entries.farsi: نعمت آرد غفلت و شكر انتباه / ‏صيد نعمت كن به دام شكر شاه
entries.english: Bounty produces heedlessness, and thanksgiving alertness: hunt after bounty with the snare of thanksgiving to the King.

entries.farsi: سير نوشى از طعام و نقل حق / ‏تا رود از تو شكم خوارى و دق
entries.english: The bounty of thanksgiving will make you contented and princely, so that you will bestow a hundred bounties on the poor.

entries.farsi: نعمت شكرت كند پر چشم و مير / تا كنى صد نعمت ايثار فقير
entries.english: You will eat your fill of the delicacies and dessert of God, so that hunger and begging will depart from you.

sections.title_fa: ‏منع كردن منكران انبيا را عليهم السلام از نصيحت كردن و حجت آوردن جبريانه
sections.title_en: How the unbelievers stopped the prophets, on whom be peace, from giving admonition and brought forward Necessitarian arguments.

entries.farsi: قوم گفتند اى نصوحان بس بود / اين چه گفتيد ار درين ده كس بود
entries.english: The people said, “O admonishers, what you have said is enough, if there be any one in this village.

entries.farsi: قفل بر دلهاى ما بنهاد حق / ‏كس نداند برد بر خالق سبق
entries.english: God has set a lock upon our hearts; none can prevail against the Creator.

entries.farsi: نقش ما اين كرد آن تصويرگر / اين نخواهد شد به گفت‏وگو دگر
entries.english: That Artist made the picture of us to be this: this will not be altered by talking.

entries.farsi: سنگ را صد سال گويى لعل شو / كهنه را صد سال گويى باش نو
entries.english: A hundred years you may tell the pebble to become a ruby, a hundred years you may tell the old to become new;

entries.farsi: خاك را گويى صفات آب گير / آب را گويى عسل شو يا كه شير
entries.english: You may tell earth to assume the qualities of water; you may tell water to become honey or milk—

entries.farsi: آسمان را داد دوران و صفا / آب و گل را تيره رويى و نما
entries.english: He is the Creator of the heavens and the heavenly ones, the Creator of water and earth and earthly beings.

entries.farsi: خالق افلاک او و افلاکيان / ‏خالق آب و تراب و خاكيان
entries.english: To heaven He gave its circling motion and its purity; to water and earth its dark appearance and its growth.

entries.farsi: قسمتى كرده ست هر يك را رهى / ‏كى كهى گردد به جهدى چون كهى
entries.english: How can heaven choose turbidity? How can earth and water buy purity?

entries.farsi: كى تواند آسمان دردى گزيد / كى تواند آب و گل صفوت خريد
entries.english: To everyone He has allotted a certain course: how should a mountain by any effort become as a straw?”

sections.title_fa: جواب انبيا عليهم السلام مر جبريان را
sections.title_en: The answer of the prophets, on whom be peace, to the Necessitarians.

entries.farsi: انبيا گفتند آرى آفريد / وصفهايى كه نتان ز آن سر كشيد
entries.english: The prophets said, “Yes: He has created some qualities from which it is impossible to withdraw one’s self,

entries.farsi: و آفريد او وصفهاى عارضى / ‏كه كسى مبغوض مى‏گردد رضى
entries.english: And He has created qualities accidental, so that a hated person becomes acceptable.

entries.farsi: سنگ را گويى كه زر شو بى‏هده‏ست / ‏مس را گويى كه زر شو راه هست
entries.english: If you bid a stone become gold, it is futile; if you bid copper become gold, the way exists.

entries.farsi: ريگ را گويى كه گل شو عاجز است / ‏خاك را گويى كه گل شو جايز است
entries.english: If you bid sand become clay, it is incapable; if you bid earth become clay that is possible.

entries.farsi: رنجها داده ست كان را چاره نيست / ‏آن به مثل لنگى و فطس و عمى است
entries.english: He has given maladies for which there is no remedy, such as lameness, flatness of the nose, and blindness;

entries.farsi: رنجها داده ست كان را چاره هست / آن به مثل لقوه و درد سر است
entries.english: He has given maladies for which there is a remedy, such as facial paralysis and headache.

entries.farsi: اين دواها ساخت بهر ائتالف / ‏نيست اين درد و دواها از گزاف
entries.english: These medicines He has made for the sake of harmony: these maladies and medicines are not in vain.

entries.farsi: بلكه اغلب رنجها را چاره هست / ‏چون به جد جويى بيايد آن به دست
entries.english: No, most maladies have a cure: when you seek in earnest, it will come to hand.”

sections.title_fa: مكرر كردن كافران حجتهاى جبريانه را
sections.title_en: How the infidels repeated the Necessitarian arguments.

entries.farsi: قوم گفتند اى گروه اين رنج ما / نيست ز آن رنجى كه بپذيرد دوا
entries.english: The people said, “O company, this malady of ours is not one of those that admit of cure.

entries.farsi: سالها گفتيد زين افسون و پند / سخت‏تر مى‏گشت ز آن هر لحظه بند
entries.english: For years ye uttered spells and admonitions of this kind, and by them our bondage was made sorer every moment.

entries.farsi: گر دوا را اين مرض قابل بدى / ‏آخر از وى ذره‏اى زايل شدى
entries.english: If this disease were susceptible of cure, some particle of it would at last have been removed.

entries.farsi: سده چون شد آب نايد در جگر / گر خورد دريا رود جايى دگر
entries.english: When hepatitis occurs, water does not enter the liver: if he should drink up the sea, it would go somewhere else;

entries.farsi: الجرم آماس گيرد دست و پا / تشنگى را نشكند آن استقا
entries.english: Consequently the hands and feet become swollen: that water-drinking does not defeat the thirst.”

sections.title_fa: باز جواب انبيا عليهم السلام ايشان را
sections.title_en: How the prophets, on whom be peace, answered them again.

entries.farsi: انبيا گفتند نوميدى بد است / ‏فضل و رحمتهاى بارى بى‏حد است
entries.english: The prophets said, “Despair is wicked: the grace and the mercies of the Creator are infinite.

entries.farsi: از چنين محسن نشايد نااميد / دست در فتراك اين رحمت زنيد
entries.english: It is not proper to despair of such a Benefactor: cling to the saddle- strap of this Mercy.

entries.farsi: اى بسا كارا كه اول صعب گشت / ‏بعد از آن بگشاده شد سختى گذشت
entries.english: Oh, many a plight became hard in the beginning, afterwards it was relieved, and the hardship passed away.

entries.farsi: بعد نوميدى بسى اوميدهاست / ‏از پس ظلمت بسى خورشيدهاست
entries.english: 2925. After despair there are many hopes; after darkness there are many suns.

entries.farsi: خود گرفتم كه شما سنگين شديد / قفلها بر گوش و بر دل بر زديد
entries.english: I grant indeed that ye have become stony and have put locks upon your ears and hearts;

entries.farsi: هيچ ما را با قبولى كار نيست / ‏كار ما تسليم و فرمان كردنى است
entries.english: We have nothing to do with any acceptance: our business is to resign ourselves and fulfill His command.

entries.farsi: او بفرمودستمان اين بندگى / ‏نيست ما را از خود اين گويندگى
entries.english: He has commanded us this service: we have not this office of pro­ claimer from ourselves.

entries.farsi: جان براى امر او داريم ما / گر به ريگى گويد او كاريم ما
entries.english: We possess life for the purpose of the command of God: if He bid us in a tract of sand, we sow

entries.farsi: غير حق جان نبى را يار نيست / ‏با قبول و رد خلقش كار نيست
entries.english: 2930. The prophet’s soul has no friend except God: he has nothing to do with the acceptance or rejection of the people.

entries.farsi: مزد تبليغ رسالاتش از اوست / ‏زشت و دشمن رو شديم از بهر دوست
entries.english: The reward for delivering His messages comes from Him: we have become hateful and wear the aspect of enemies for the Beloved’s sake.

entries.farsi: ما بر اين درگه ملوالن نيستيم / ‏تا ز بعد راه هر جا بيستيم
entries.english: At this Portal we are not weary, so that we should halt everywhere because of the distance of the way.

entries.farsi: دل فرو بسته و ملول آن كس بود / كز فراق يار در محبس بود
entries.english: Oppressed in heart and weary is that one who is in prison through being parted from the Friend.

entries.farsi: دل بر و مطلوب با ما حاضر است / ‏در نثار رحمتش جان شاكر است
entries.english: The Heart-ravisher and Desired One is present with us: amidst the largesse of His mercy our souls are giving thanks.

entries.farsi: در دل ما الله‏زار و گلشنى است / ‏پيرى و پژمردگى را راه نيست
entries.english: 2935. In our hearts is an anemone-field and rose-garden: there is no way for old age and decay;

entries.farsi: دايما تر و جوانيم و لطيف / ‏تازه و شيرين و خندان و ظريف
entries.english: We are ever fresh and young and gracious, unfading and sweet and laughing and debonair.

entries.farsi: پيش ما صد سال و يك ساعت يكى است / ‏كه دراز و كوته از ما منفكى است
entries.english: To us a hundred years are the same as a single hour, for long and short is a thing disjoined from us.

entries.farsi: آن دراز و كوتهى در جسمهاست / ‏آن دراز و كوته اندر جان كجاست
entries.english: That length and shortness is in bodies: where is that long and short in the soul?

entries.farsi: سيصد و نه سال آن اصحاب كهف / ‏پيششان يك روز بى‏اندوه و لهف
entries.english: The three hundred and nine years of the Men of the Cave seemed to them one day without grief and woe;

entries.farsi: و آن گهى بنمودشان يك روز هم / ‏كه به تن باز آمد ارواح از عدم
entries.english: And then did it seem to them one day even, when their spirits came back from non-existence into their bodies.

entries.farsi: چون نباشد روز و شب با ماه و سال / ‏كى بود سيرى و پيرى و مالل
entries.english: When there is no day and night and month and year, how should there be satiety and old age and weariness?

entries.farsi: در گلستان عدم چون بى‏خودى است / ‏مستى از سغراق لطف ايزدى است
entries.english: Since there is selflessness in the rose-garden of non-existence, there is intoxication caused by the goblet of Divine grace.

entries.farsi: لم يذق لم يدر هر كس كاو نخورد / كى به وهم آرد جعل انفاس ورد
entries.english: Any one that has not drunk has not tasted does not know: how should the dung-beetle conceive the breaths of the rose?

entries.farsi: دوزخ اندر وهم چون آرد بهشت / ‏هيچ تابد روى خوب از خوك زشت
entries.english: It is not conceivable: if it were conceivable, it would become non-existent, like objects of conception.

entries.farsi: نيست موهوم ار بدى موهوم آن / ‏همچو موهومان شدى معدوم آن
entries.english: How should Hell conceive Paradise? Does a beauteous face shine at all from an ugly pig?

entries.farsi: هين گلوى خود مبر هان اى مهان / ‏اين چنين لقمه رسيده تا دهان
entries.english: Listen; do not cut your own throat! Take heed, O despicable one, such a morsel as this has reached your mouth.

entries.farsi: راههاى صعب پايان برده‏ايم / ‏ره بر اهل خويش آسان كرده‏ايم
entries.english: We have brought the hard ways to an end; we have made the way easy for our own people.”

sections.title_fa: ‏مكرر كردن قوم اعتراض ترجيه بر انبيا عليهم السلام
sections.title_en: How the people repeated their resistance to the hope against the prophets, on whom be peace.

entries.farsi: قوم گفتند ار شما سعد خوديد / نحس ماييد و ضديد و مرتديد
entries.english: The people said, “If you bring good luck to yourselves, you are ill-starred for us and are opposed and rejected.

entries.farsi: جان ما فارغ بد از انديشه‏ها / در غم افكنديد ما را و عنا
entries.english: Our souls were free from cares: you have cast us into grief and trouble.

entries.farsi: ذوق جمعيت كه بود و اتفاق / ‏شد ز فال زشتتان صد افتراق
entries.english: Through your evil omen the delightful concord and agreement that existed has been turned into a hundred separations.

entries.farsi: طوطى نقل شكر بوديم ما / مرغ مرگ انديش گشتيم از شما
entries.english: We were parrots eating sugar for dessert; through you we have become birds that meditate on death.

entries.farsi: هر كجا افسانه‏ى غم گسترى است / ‏هر كجا آوازه‏ى مستنكرى است
entries.english: Wherever is a grief-spreading tale, wherever is an odious rumour,

entries.farsi: هر كجا اندر جهان فال بذى است / ‏هر كجا مسخى نكالى مأخذى است
entries.english: Wherever in the world is an evil omen, wherever is a monstrous transformation, a terrible punishment, an infliction of chastisement—

entries.farsi: در مثال قصه و فال شماست / ‏در غم انگيزى شما را مشتهاست
entries.english: In the parable of your story and in your evil omen: you have an appetite for rousing grief.”

sections.title_fa: ‏باز جواب انبيا عليهم السلام
sections.title_en: How the prophets, on whom be peace, answered them once more.

entries.farsi: انبيا گفتند فال زشت و بد / از ميان جانتان دارد مدد
entries.english: The prophets said, “The foul and evil omen has its support from within your souls.

entries.farsi: گر تو جايى خفته باشى با خطر / اژدها در قصد تو از سوى سر
entries.english: If you are asleep in a perilous place, and a dragon is approaching you from a spot close at hand,

entries.farsi: مهربانى مر ترا آگاه كرد / كه بجه زود ار نه اژدرهات خورد
entries.english: And a kindly person has made you aware, saying, ‘Jump up quickly, or else the dragon will devour you’—

entries.farsi: تو بگويى فال بد چون مى‏زنى / ‏فال چه بر جه ببين در روشنى
entries.english: If you say, ‘Why are you uttering an evil omen?’ ‘What omen? Jump up and see in the light of day.

entries.farsi: از ميان فال بد من خود ترا / مى‏رهانم مى‏برم سوى سرا
entries.english: I myself will deliver you from the midst of the evil omen and will take you home.’

entries.farsi: چون نبى آگه كننده ست از نهان / ‏كاو بديد آن چه نديد اهل جهان
entries.english: He is one that acquaints with things hidden, like the prophet who has seen what the people of this world have not seen.

entries.farsi: گر طبيبى گويدت غوره مخور / كه چنين رنجى بر آرد شور و شر
entries.english: If a physician says to you, ‘Do not eat unripe grapes, for such an illness will produce trouble and mischief,’

entries.farsi: تو بگويى فال بد چون مى‏زنى / ‏پس تو ناصح را موثم مى‏كنى
entries.english: And if you say, ‘Why are you uttering an evil omen?’— Then you are making out your sincere adviser to be culpable.

entries.farsi: ور منجم گويدت كامروز هيچ / ‏آن چنان كارى مكن اندر بسيچ
entries.english: And if an astrologer say to you, ‘By no means set about such an affair to-day,’

entries.farsi: صد ره ار بينى دروغ اخترى / ‏يك دو باره راست آيد مى‏خرى
entries.english: Though you see the falsehood of the astrologer a hundred times, it come true once or twice, you are eager to trust him.

entries.farsi: اين نجوم ما نشد هرگز خالف / ‏صحتش چون ماند از تو در غالف
entries.english: These stars of ours are never at variance: how does their truth remain concealed from you?

entries.farsi: آن طبيب و آن منجم از گمان / ‏مى‏كنند آگاه و ما خود از عيان
entries.english: The physician and the astrologer inform from opinion, and verily we from clairvoyance:

entries.farsi: دود مى‏بينيم و آتش از كران / ‏حمله مى‏آرد به سوى منكران
entries.english: We behold the smoke and fire rushing from afar towards the unbelievers.

entries.farsi: تو همى‏گويى خمش كن زين مقال / ‏كه زيان ماست قال شوم فال
entries.english: You are saying, ‘be silent from these words, for the words of evil omen are hurtful to us.’”

entries.farsi: اى كه نصح ناصحان را نشنوى / ‏فال بد با تست هر جا مى‏روى
entries.english: O you who listen not unto the admonition of the admonishers, the evil omen is with you wherever you go.

entries.farsi: افعيى بر پشت تو بر مى‏رود / او ز بامى بيندش آگه كند
entries.english: A viper is walking on your back: he sees it from a roof and makes aware;

entries.farsi: گويى‏اش خاموش غمگينم مكن / ‏گويد او خوش باش خود رفت آن سخن
entries.english: You say to him, “Hush! Do not vex me”: he says, “Be happy! Truly, the words have gone.”

entries.farsi: چون زند افعى دهان بر گردنت / ‏تلخ گردد جمله شادى جستنت
entries.english: When the viper darts its mouth at your neck, all your desire for happiness is made bitter.

entries.farsi: پس بدو گويى همين بود اى فالن / ‏چون بندريدى گريبان در فغان
entries.english: Then you say to him, “O so-and-so was this all? Why didn’t you tear your collar in outcry?

entries.farsi: يا ز بالايم تو سنگى مى‏زدى / ‏تا مرا آن جد نمودى و بدى
entries.english: Or why weren’t you throwing a stone at me from above, in order that that grave calamity and misfortune might be shown to me?”

entries.farsi: او بگويد ز انكه مى‏آزرده‏اى / ‏تو بگويى نيك شادم كرده‏اى
entries.english: He says, “Because you were annoyed”; you say, “You have made me very happy!”

entries.farsi: گفت من كردم جوانمردى به پند / تا رهانم من ترا زين خشك بند
entries.english: He says, “I bestowed counsel generously, that I might deliver you from this sterile bondage.

entries.farsi: از لئيمى حق آن نشناختى / ‏مايه‏ى ايذا و طغيان ساختى
entries.english: From vileness you acknowledged no obligation for that: you made a source of injury and insolence.”

entries.farsi: اين بود خوى لئيمان دنى / ‏بد كند با تو چو نيكويى كنى
entries.english: This is the nature of base villains: he does evil to you when you do good.

entries.farsi: نفس را زين صبر مى‏كن منحنيش / كه لئيم است و نسازد نيكوييش
entries.english: As for the fleshly soul, bend it double by means of this renunciation, for it is vile, and kindness suits it not.

entries.farsi: با كريمى گر كنى احسان سزد / مر يكى را او عوض هفصد دهد
entries.english: If you show beneficence to a noble man, it is fitting: he will give seven hundred in exchange for every one;

entries.farsi: با لئيمى چون كنى قهر و جفا / بنده‏اى گردد ترا بس با وفا
entries.english: When you treat a villain with violence and cruelty, he becomes a very faithful servant to you.

entries.farsi: كافران كارند در نعمت جفا / باز در دوزخ نداشان ربنا
entries.english: The infidels in prosperity sow cruelty; again in Hell their cry is “O Lord!”

sections.title_fa: ًحكمت آفريدن دوزخ آن جهان و زندان اين جهان تا معبد متكبران باشد كه ائْتِيا طَوْعاً أَوْ كَرْها
sections.title_en: The wisdom of having created Hell in the world hereafter and the prison of the present world to the end that they may be places of worship for the arrogant: “Come willingly or unwillingly.”

entries.farsi: كه لئيمان در جفا صافى شوند / چون وفا بينند خود جافى شوند
entries.english: For in cruelty the vile are purified; when they receive kindness, they themselves become cruel.

entries.farsi: مسجد طاعاتشان پس دوزخ است / هست زندان صومعه‏ى دزد و لئيم
entries.english: Therefore Hell is the mosque where they perform their devotions: a trap is the fetter for a wild bird.

entries.farsi: كاندر او ذاكر شود حق را مقيم‏ / چون عبادت بود مقصود از بشر
entries.english: Prison is the cloister of the thief and villain, that there he may be constantly mindful of God.

entries.farsi: شد عبادتگاه گردن كش سقر / آدمى را هست در هر كار دست
entries.english: Inasmuch as Divine worship was the object of mankind, Hellfire was made the place of worship for the rebellious.

entries.farsi: ليك ازو مقصود اين خدمت بده ست‏ / ما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَ الإِْنْسَ اين بخوان
entries.english: Man has the power in everything, but this service has been the object of him.

entries.farsi: جز عبادت نيست مقصود از جهان‏ / گر چه مقصود از كتاب آن فن بود
entries.english: “I did not create the Jinn and mankind.” Recite this. The object of the world is nothing but Divine worship.

entries.farsi: گر تواش بالش كنى هم مى‏شود / ليك ازو مقصود اين بالش نبود
entries.english: Though the object of a book is the science, if you make it a pillow, it will become that too;

entries.farsi: علم بود و دانش و ارشاد و سود / گر تو ميخى ساختى شمشير را
entries.english: But this pillow was not its object: it was learning and knowledge and right guidance and profit.

entries.farsi: بر گزيدى بر ظفر ادبار را / گر چه مقصود از بشر علم و هدى است
entries.english: If you have made the sword a tent-pin, you have preferred defeat to victory.

entries.farsi: ليك هر يك آدمى را معبدى است‏ / معبد مرد كريم أكرمته
entries.english: Although the object of Man is knowledge and to be rightly directed, every man has a particular place of worship

entries.farsi: معبد مرد لئيم أسقمته‏ / مر لئيمان را بزن تا سر نهند
entries.english: The place where the noble man worships is your treating him with kindness; the place where the vile man worships is your making him sick.

entries.farsi: مر كرميان را بده تا بر دهند / الجرم حق هر دو مسجد آفريد
entries.english: Smite the vile, that they may bow their heads; give to the noble, that they may yield fruit.

entries.farsi: دوزخ آنها را و اينها را مزيد / ساخت موسى قدس در باب صغير
entries.english: Necessarily God has created a mosque for each of the two — Hell for those, and increase for these.

entries.farsi: تا فرود آرند سر قوم زحير / ز انكه جباران بدند و سر فراز
entries.english: Moses built the Bab-i Saghir at Jerusalem, in order that the people tormented might lower their heads,

entries.farsi: دوزخ آن باب صغير است و نياز / آن چنان كه حق ز گوشت و استخوان
entries.english: Because they were insolent and arrogant. Hell is that Bab-i Saghir and (place of) humiliation.

sections.title_fa: بيان آن كه حق تعالى صورت ملوك را سبب مسخر كردن جباران كه مسخر حق نباشند ساخته است چنان كه موسى عليه السلام باب صغير ساخت بر ربض قدس جهت ركوع جباران بنى اسرائيل ٌوقت در آمدن كه ادْخُلُوا الْبابَ سُ جَّداً وَ قُولُوا حِطَّة

sections.title_en: Explaining how God most High has made the bodily form of kings a means of subduing the insolent (sinners) who are not subject to God, just as Moses, on whom be peace, built the Bab-i Saghir in the wall of Jerusalem in order that the insolent (and wicked) men among the Israelites might bow low when they entered in, (according to the text),
“Enter the gate, prostrating yourselves, and say ‘hittatun.’”

entries.farsi: چون كه سجده‏ى كبريا را دشمنند / ساخت سرگين دانكى محراب‏شان
entries.english: Likewise God has built a Bab-i Saghir from the flesh and bones of kings. Take heed!

entries.farsi: ‏از شهان باب صغيرى ساخت هان / اهل دنيا سجده‏ى ايشان كنند
entries.english: The people of this world make prostration before them, since they are opposed to prostration before the Divine Majesty.

entries.farsi: ‏نام آن محراب مير و پهلوان / اليق اين حضرت پاكى نه‏ايد
entries.english: He has made a little dunghill their mihrab: the name of that mihrab is “prince” and “paladin.”

entries.farsi: نيشكر پاكان شما خالى نبيد / آن سگان را اين خسان خاضع شوند
entries.english: You are not fit for this holy Presence: holy men are the sugarcane; you are the empty reed.

entries.farsi: شير را عار است كاو را بگروند / گربه باشد شحنه‏ى هر موش خو
entries.english: These vile wretches grovel before those curs; it is a disgrace to the lion that they should be complaisant to him.

entries.farsi: موش كه بود تا ز شيران ترسد او / خوف ايشان از كالب حق بود
entries.english: The cat is the overseer of every mouse-natured one: who is the mouse that it should be afraid of the lions?

entries.farsi: خوفشان كى ز آفتاب حق بود / ربي األعلى است ورد آن مهان
entries.english: Their fear is of the dogs of God: how should they have fear of the Sun of God?

entries.farsi: ‏رب ادنى در خور اين ابلهان / موش كى ترسد ز شيران مصاف
entries.english: The litany of those great ones is “my Lord the most High”; “my lord the most low” is suitable to these fools.

entries.farsi: ‏بلكه آن آهوتگان مشك ناف / رو به پيش كاسه ليس اى ديگ ليس
entries.english: How should the mouse fear the lions of the battle-field? No, those who have the speed and the musk-bag of the deer

entries.farsi: ‏توش خداوند و ولى نعمت نويس / بس كن ار شرحى بگويم دور دست
entries.english: O licker of pots, go to him that licks basins and write him down as your lord and benefactor!

entries.farsi: ‏خشم گيرد مير و هم داند كه هست / حاصل اين آمد كه بد كن اى كريم
entries.english: Enough! If I give a far-reaching exposition, the prince will be angered; and besides he knows that it is.

entries.farsi: چون لئيمان نفس بد كفران كند / زين سبب بد كه اهل محنت شاكرند
entries.english: The upshot is this:—“O noble man, do evil to the vile, that the villain may lay his neck.”

entries.farsi: ‏با لئيمان تا نهد گردن لئيم / با لئيم نفس چون احسان كند
entries.english: When he deals kindly with the villain, his soul, the wicked soul shows ingratitude, like the vile.

entries.farsi: اهل نعمت طاغى‏اند و ماكرند / هست طاغى بگلر زرين قبا
entries.english: It was on this account that the afflicted are thankful, the fortunate are rebellious and deceitful.

entries.farsi: هست شاكر خسته‏ى صاحب عبا / شكر كى رويد ز امالك و نعم
entries.english: The bey with his gold-embroidered coat is rebellious; the distressed wearer of a coarse woollen cloak (aba) is thankful.

entries.farsi: ‏شكر مى‏رويد ز بلوى و سقم / صوفيى بر ميخ روزى سفره ديد
entries.english: How should thankfulness grow from possessions and riches? Thankfulness grows from tribulation and sickness.

sections.title_fa: ‏قصه‏ى عشق صوفى بر سفره‏ى تهى
sections.title_en: Story of the Sufis being enamoured of the empty food-wallet

entries.farsi: چرخ مى‏زد جامه‏ها را مى‏دريد / بانگ مى‏زد نك نواى بى‏نوا
entries.english: One day a Sufi espied a food-wallet on a nail: he began to whirl and rend his garments,

entries.farsi: قحطها و دردها را نك دوا / چون كه دود و شور او بسيار شد
entries.english: Crying, “Lo, the food of the foodless! Lo, the remedy for famines and pangs!”

entries.farsi: تاى چندى مست و بى‏خود مى‏شدند / بو الفضولى گفت صوفى را كه چيست
entries.english: When his smoke and tumult waxed great, every one that was a Sufi joined him.

entries.farsi: هر كه صوفى بود با او يار شد / كخ كخى و هاى و هويى مى‏زدند
entries.english: They were shouting and shrieking: several were becoming intoxicated and beside themselves.

entries.farsi: ‏سفره‏ى آويخته و ز نان تهى است / گفت رو رو نقش بى‏معنيستى
entries.english: An idle busybody said to the Sufi, “What is the matter? A food-wallet hung, and it is empty of bread!”

entries.farsi: تو بجو هستى كه عاشق نيستى / عشق نان بى‏نان غذاى عاشق است
entries.english: He said, “Go,go! You are a form without spirit: do you seek existence, for you are no lover.”

entries.farsi: ‏بند هستى نيست هر كاو صادق است / عاشقان را كار نبود با وجود
entries.english: The lover’s food is love of the bread, without the bread: no one that is sincere is in thrall to existence.

entries.farsi: دست نى و گو ز ميدان مى‏برند / آن فقيرى كاو ز معنى بوى يافت
entries.english: Lovers have nothing to do with existence: lovers have the interest without the capital.

entries.farsi: عاشقان را هست بى‏سرمايه سود / بال نى و گرد عالم مى‏پرند
entries.english: They have no wings, and they fly round the world; they have no hands, and they carry off the ball from the polo-field.

entries.farsi: ‏دست ببريده همى زنبيل بافت / عاشقان اندر عدم خيمه زدند
entries.english: That dervish who scented Reality used to weave baskets though his hand had been cut off.

entries.farsi: چون عدم يك رنگ و نفس واحدند / شير خواره كى شناسد ذوق لوت
entries.english: Lovers have pitched their tents in non-existence: they are of one colour and one essence, like non-existence.

entries.farsi: ‏مر پرى را بوى باشد لوت و پوت / آدمى كى بو برد از بوى او
entries.english: How should the sucking babe know the taste of viands? To the Jinn scent is meat and drink.

entries.farsi: چون كه خوى اوست ضد خوى او / يابد از بو آن پرى بوى كش
entries.english: How shall a human being scent his scent, inasmuch as his nature is contrary to his nature?

entries.farsi: ‏تو نيابى آن ز صد من لوت خوش / پيش قبطى خون بود آن آب نيل
entries.english: That scent-inhaling Jinn gains from the scent: you will not gain that from a hundred kilograms of sweet dainties.

entries.farsi: ‏آب باشد پيش سبطى جميل / جاده باشد بحر ز اسرائيليان
entries.english: To the Copt the water of the Nile is blood; to the goodly Israelite it is water.

entries.farsi: ‏غرقه‏گه باشد ز فرعون عوان
entries.english: By the Israelites the sea is a highway; by the ruffian Pharaoh it is a drowning-place.

sections.title_fa: مخصوص بودن يعقوب عليه السلام به چشيدن جام حق از روى يوسف و كشيدن بوى حق از بوى يوسف و حرمان برادران و غيرهم از اين هر دو
sections.title_en: How Jacob, on whom be peace, was privileged to taste the cup of God from the face of Joseph, and inhale the scent of God from the scent of Joseph; and the exclusion of his brethren and others from both these.

entries.farsi: آن چه يعقوب از رخ يوسف بديد / خاص او بد آن به اخوان كى رسيد
entries.english: That which Jacob experienced from the face of Joseph was peculiar to him: when did that come to his brethren?

entries.farsi: اين ز عشقش خويش در چه مى‏كند / و آن به كين از بهر او چه مى‏كند
entries.english: This one, from love of him, puts himself in the pit, while that one digs a pit for him in hatred.

entries.farsi: سفره‏ى او پيش اين از نان تهى است / ‏پيش يعقوب است پر كاو مشتهى است
entries.english: In the sight of this one his food-wallet is empty of bread; in the sight of Jacob it is full, for he desires eagerly.

entries.farsi: روى ناشسته نبيند روى حور / ال صالة گفت إال بالطهور
entries.english: None with face unwashed beholds the faces of the houris: he said, “There is no ritual prayer without the ablution.”

entries.farsi: عشق باشد لوت و پوت جانها / جوع از اين روى است قوت جانها
entries.english: Love is the meat and drink of souls; hunger, from this point of view, is the food of souls.

entries.farsi: جوع يوسف بود آن يعقوب را / بوى نانش مى‏رسيد از دور جا
entries.english: Jacob had hunger for Joseph; the smell of the bread was reaching him from afar.

entries.farsi: آن كه بستد پيرهن را مى‏شتافت / ‏بوى پيراهان يوسف مى‏نيافت
entries.english: He that took the shirt was hastening and was not perceiving the scent of Joseph’s shirt,

entries.farsi: اى بسا عالم ز دانش بى‏نصيب / ‏حافظ علم است آن كس نى حبيب
entries.english: While he that was a hundred leagues from that quarter he smelled the perfume, since he was Jacob.

entries.farsi: و انكه صد فرسنگ ز آن سو بود او / چون كه بد يعقوب مى‏بوييد بو
entries.english: Oh, there is many a learned man that has no profit of knowledge: that person is one who commits knowledge to memory, not one who loves.

entries.farsi: مستمع از وى همى‏يابد مشام / گر چه باشد مستمع از جنس عام
entries.english: From him the hearer perceives the scent, though the hearer is of the common sort,

entries.farsi: ز انكه پيراهن به دستش عاريه است / ‏چون به دست آن نخاسى جاريه است
entries.english: Because the shirt in his hand is a borrowed thing, like a slave-girl in the hands of a slave-dealer.

entries.farsi: جاريه پيش نخاسى سرسرى است / ‏در كف او از براى مشترى است
entries.english: The slave-girl is useless to the slave-dealer: she is in his hands for the sake of the purchaser.

entries.farsi: قسمت حق است روزى دادنى / ‏هر يكى را سوى ديگر راه نى
entries.english: The dispensation of God is a bestowal of the allotted portion: no one’s has access to another.

entries.farsi: يك خيال نيك باغ آن شده / ‏يك خيال زشت راه اين زده
entries.english: A good intention becomes that man’s garden; an ugly intention waylays this man.

entries.farsi: آن خدايى كز خيالى باغ ساخت / ‏و ز خيالى دوزخ و جاى گداخت
entries.english: That Lord who has made from one fancy the garden and from one fancy Hell and the place of melting—

entries.farsi: پس كه داند راه گلشنهاى او / پس كه داند جاى گلخنهاى او
entries.english: Then who should know the way to His rose gardens? Then who should know the way to His furnaces?

entries.farsi: ديدبان دل نبيند در مجال / كز كدامني ركن جان آيد خيال‏
entries.english: The sentry of the heart, while on his round, does not see from what corner of the soul the image comes.

entries.farsi: گر بديدى مطلعش را ز احتيال / ‏بند كردى راه هر ناخوش خيال
entries.english: If he saw its rising-place, he would contrive to bar the way to every unlovely thought;

entries.farsi: كى رسد جاسوس را آن جا قدم / ‏كه بود مرصاد و در بند عدم
entries.english: How should the foot of the scout reach that spot?— for it is the watchtower and mountain-fortress of Non-existence.

entries.farsi: دامن فضلش به كف كن كوروار / قبض اعمى اين بود اى شهريار
entries.english: Blindly lay hold of the skirt of His grace: this is the blind man’s taking possession the land, O king.

entries.farsi: دامن او امر و فرمان وى است / ‏نيك بختى كه تقى جان وى است
entries.english: His skirt is His command and behest: fortunate is he to whom piety is his soul.

entries.farsi: آن يكى در مرغزار و جوى آب / ‏و آن يكى پهلوى او اندر عذاب
entries.english: The one is in meadows and water-brooks, while the other one beside him is in torment.

entries.farsi: او عجب مانده كه ذوق اين ز چيست / ‏و آن عجب مانده كه اين در حبس كيست
entries.english: He remains in wonderment, saying, “Wherefore is this man’s delight?” and the other remains in wonderment, saying, “In whose prison is this man?

entries.farsi: هين چرا خشكى كه اينجا چشمه‏هاست / ‏هين چرا زردى كه اينجا صد دواست
entries.english: Listen, why are you parched?—for here are fountains. Listen, why are you pale?—for here are a hundred remedies.

entries.farsi: همنشينا هين در آ اندر چمن / ‏گويد اى جان من نيارم آمدن
entries.english: Listen, neighbour, and come into the garden!” He says, “O soul, I cannot come.”

sections.title_fa: ‏حكايت امير و غالمش كه نماز باره بود و انس عظيم داشت در نماز و مناجات با حق
sections.title_en: Story of the Amir and his slave who was very fond of the ritual prayer and had a great joy in the ritual prayer and in communing with God

entries.farsi: مير شد محتاج گرمابه سحر / بانگ زد سنقر هال بردار سر
entries.english: At dawn the Amir wanted the hot bath: he shouted, “Ho Sunqur, rouse yourself!

entries.farsi: سنقر آن دم طاس و منديلى نكو / بر گرفت و رفت با او دو به دو
entries.english: Get from Altun the basin and the napkin and the clay that we may go to the hot bath, O indispensable one.”

entries.farsi: طاس و منديل و گل از التون بگير / تا به گرمابه رويم اى ناگزير
entries.english: Sunqur at that moment took up the basin and a fine napkin and set out with him—the two together.

entries.farsi: مسجدى بر ره بد و بانگ صال / بود سنقر سخت مولع در نماز
entries.english: There was a mosque on the road, and the call to prayer came publicly into Sunqur’s ear.

entries.farsi: گفت اى مير من اى بنده نواز / تو بر اين دكان زمانى صبر كن
entries.english: Sunqur was very fond of the ritual prayer: he said, “O my Amir, O kind master,

entries.farsi: ‏تا گذارم فرض و خوانم لَمْ يكن / چون امام و قوم بيرون آمدند
entries.english: Stay patiently for a while on this bench, that I may perform the obligatory prayers and may recite lam yakun.”

entries.farsi: از نماز و وردها فارغ شدند / سنقر آن جا ماند تا نزديك چاشت
entries.english: When the Imam and the people had come forth and finished the prayers and litanies,

entries.farsi: ‏مير سنقر را زمانى چشم داشت / گفت اى سنقر چرا نايى برون
entries.english: Sunqur remained there till near the forenoon: the Amir awaited him for some time;

entries.farsi: ‏گفت مى‏نگذاردم اين ذو فنون / صبر كن نك آمدم اى روشنى
entries.english: He said, “O Sunqur, why don’t you come out?” He replied, “This artful One will not let me.

entries.farsi: ‏نيستم غافل كه در گوش منى / هفت نوبت صبر كرد و بانگ كرد
entries.english: Have patience! Behold, I come, O light! I am not heedless, for you are in my ear.”

entries.farsi: تا كه عاجز گشت از تيباش مرد / پاسخش اين بود مى‏نگذاردم
entries.english: Seven times in succession did he show patience and shout— till at last the man was reduced to despair by his trifling.

entries.farsi: ‏تا برون آيم هنوز اى محترم / گفت آخر مسجد اندر كس نماند
entries.english: His reply was this— “He will not let me come out yet, O revered.”

entries.farsi: كيت وا مى‏دارد آن جا كت نشاند / گفت آن كه بسته استت از برون
entries.english: He said, “Why, there is no one left in the mosque. Who is detaining you there? Who has made you sit?”

entries.farsi: ‏بسته است او هم مرا در اندرون / آن كه نگذارد ترا كايى درون
entries.english: He said, “He who has chained you outside has chained me too inside.

entries.farsi: ‏مى‏نبگذارد مرا كايم برون / آن كه نگذارد كز اين سو پا نهى
entries.english: He who will not let you come in will not let me come out.

entries.farsi: خاكيان را بحر نگذارد درون / اصل ماهى آب و حيوان از گل است
entries.english: He who will not let you set foot in this direction has chained the foot of this slave in this direction.”

entries.farsi: ‏او بدين سو بست پاى اين رهى / ماهيان را بحر نگذارد برون
entries.english: The sea does not let the fish out; the sea does not let the creatures of earth in.

entries.farsi: ‏حيله و تدبير اينجا باطل است / قفل زفت است و گشاينده خدا
entries.english: Water is the original home of the fish, and the animal is of the earth: here device and contrivance are of no avail.

entries.farsi: اين گشايش نيست جز از كبريا / چون فراموشت شود تدبير خويش
entries.english: Strong is the lock, and the opener is God: cling to resignation and acquiescence.

entries.farsi: دست در تسليم زن و اندر رضا / ذره ذره گر شود مفتاحها
entries.english: Though the atoms, one by one, should become keys, this opening is not except by the Divine Majesty.

entries.farsi: ‏يابى آن بخت جوان از پير خويش / چون فراموش خودى يادت كنند
entries.english: When you forget your own contrivance, you will gain that young fortune from your spiritual Guide.

entries.farsi: بنده گشتى آن گه آزادت كنند / انبيا گفتند با خاطر كه چند
entries.english: When you are forgetful of self, you are remembered: you have become a slave, then you are set free.

sections.title_fa: ُنوميد شدن انبيا از قبول و پذيرايى منكران قوله حَتَّى إِذَا اسْ تَيْأَسَ الرُّسُ ل
sections.title_en: How the prophets lost hope of being accepted and approved by the unbelievers, as God has said: “Until, when the Messengers despaired…”

entries.farsi: مى‏دهيم اين را و آن را وعظ و پند / چند كوبيم آهن سردى ز غى
entries.english: The prophets said to their hearts, “How long shall we continue giving exhortation and counsel to this one and that one?

entries.farsi: ‏در دميدن در قفس هين تا به كى / جنبش خلق از قضا و وعده است
entries.english: How long shall we misguidedly beat a piece of cold iron? Listen, till when to breathe into a cage?”

entries.farsi: ‏تيزى دندان ز سوز معده است / نفس اول راند بر نفس دوم
entries.english: The motion of created beings is by Divine destiny and appointment: the sharpness of the teeth is by the burning of the stomach.

entries.farsi: ‏ماهى از سر گنده گردد نى ز دم / ليك هم مى‏دان و خر مى‏ران چو تير
entries.english: The First Soul pushed upon the second soul: a fish stinks from the head, not from the tail.

entries.farsi: چون كه بَلِّغْ گفت حق شد ناگزير / تو نمى‏دانى كز اين دو كيستى
entries.english: But, while recognising, still speed on like an arrow: since God has said, “Deliver,” there is no escape.

entries.farsi: ‏جهد كن چندان كه بينى چيستى / چون نهى بر پشت كشتى بار را
entries.english: You do not know which of these two you are: strive so long that you may discern what you are.

entries.farsi: بر توكل مى‏كنى آن كار را / تو نمى‏دانى كه از هر دو كى‏اى
entries.english: When you put a cargo on board a ship, you are making that venture on trust,

entries.farsi: ‏بر نخواهم تاخت در كشتى و يم / من در اين ره ناجى‏ام يا غرقه‏ام
entries.english: you do not know which of the two you are— whether you are drowned on the voyage or saved.

entries.farsi: ‏غرقه‏اى اندر سفر يا ناجى‏اى / گر بگويى تا ندانم من كى‏ام
entries.english: If you say, “Until I know which I am, I will not hasten on to the ship and the ocean;

entries.farsi: ‏كشف گردان كز كدامين فرقه‏ام / من نخواهم رفت اين ره با گمان
entries.english: On this voyage I am saved or drowned: reveal to which party I belong.

entries.farsi: بر اميد خشك همچون ديگران / هيچ بازرگانيى نايد ز تو
entries.english: I will not start upon this voyage with doubt and in idle hope, like the others”—

entries.farsi: ز انكه در غيب است سر اين دو رو / تاجر ترسنده طبع شيشه جان
entries.english: no traffic will be done by you, because the secret of these two aspects is in the Unseen.

entries.farsi: ‏در طلب نه سود دارد نه زيان / بل زيان دارد كه محروم است و خوار
entries.english: The merchant of timid disposition and frail spirit neither gains nor loses in his quest;

entries.farsi: نور او يابد كه باشد شعله خوار / چون كه بر بوك است جمله كارها
entries.english: No, he suffers loss, for he is deprived and despicable: he that is an eater of flames will find the light.

entries.farsi: كار دين اولى كز اين يابى رها / نيست دستورى بدين جا قرع باب
entries.english: Inasmuch as all affairs turn upon hope, the affair of religion is most worthy, for by this means you may win salvation.

entries.farsi: ‏جز اميد اللهَّ أعلم بالصواب / داعى هر پيشه اوميد است و بوك
entries.english: Here it is not permitted to knock at the door; nothing but hope: God best knows the right course.

sections.title_fa: يان آن كه ايمان مقلد خوف است و رجا
sections.title_en: Explaining how the faith of the conventional man consists in fear and hope.

entries.farsi: گر چه گردنشان ز كوشش شد چو دوك‏ / بامدادان چون سوى دكان رود
entries.english: The motive in every trade is hope and chance, even though their necks are, like a spindle, from toil.

entries.farsi: بر اميد و بوك روزى مى‏دود / بو كه روزى نبودت چون مى‏روى
entries.english: When he goes in the morning to his shop, he is running in the hope and chance of a livelihood.

entries.farsi: خوف حرمان هست تو چونى قوى‏ / خوف حرمان ازل در كسب لوت
entries.english: You have not the chance of a livelihood, why do you go? There is the fear of disappointment: how are you strong?

entries.farsi: چون نكردت سست اندر جستجوت‏ / گويى ار چه خوف حرمان هست پيش
entries.english: In earning food, how has the fear of eternal disappointment not made you feeble in your search?

entries.farsi: هست اندر كاهلى اين خوف بيش‏ / هست در كوشش اميدم بيشتر
entries.english: You will say, “Though the fear of disappointment is before, this fear is greater in idleness.

entries.farsi: دارم اندر كاهلى افزون خطر / پس چرا در كار دين اى بد گمان
entries.english: At work my hope is greater: in idleness I have more risk.”

entries.farsi: دامنت مى‏گيرد اين خوف زيان‏ / يا نديدى كه اهل اين بازار ما
entries.english: Then, O evil-thinking man, why is this fear of loss holding you back in the matter of religion?

entries.farsi: در چه سودند انبيا و اوليا / زين دكان‏رفتن چه كانشان رو نمود
entries.english: Or have not you seen in what a gainful trade the people of this bazaar of ours, the prophets and the saints, are,

entries.farsi: اندر اين بازار چون بستند سود / آتش آن را رام چون خلخال شد
entries.english: And what mines have appeared to them from this going to the shop, and how they have gotten gain in this market?

entries.farsi: بحر آن را رام شد حمال شد / هن آن را رام شد چون موم شد
entries.english: To that one the fire became submissive, like an anklet; to that one the sea became submissive and carried him on its shoulders;

entries.farsi: باد آن را بنده و محكوم شد / قوم ديگر سخت پنهان مى‏روند
entries.english: To that one the iron became submissive and wax-like; to that one the wind became a slave and subject.

sections.title_fa: بيان آن كه رسول عليه السلام فرمود ان هلل تعالى أولياء أخفياء
sections.title_en: Setting forth how the Prophet, on whom be peace, said, “Verily, God most High has friends who are concealed.”

entries.farsi: شهره‏ى خلقان ظاهر كى شوند / اين همه دارند و چشم هيچ كس
entries.english: Another party go exceedingly hidden: how should they become well-known to the people of externals?

entries.farsi: بر نيفتد بر كياشان يك نفس‏ / هم كرامتشان هم ايشان در حرم
entries.english: They possess all this, and no one’s eye falls upon their sovereignty for one moment.

entries.farsi: نامشان را نشنوند ابدال هم‏ / يا نمى‏دانى كرمهاى خدا
entries.english: Both their miracles and they are in the sanctuary: even the Abdal do not hear their names.

entries.farsi: كاو ترا مى‏خواند آن سو كه بيا / شش جهت عالم همه اكرام اوست
entries.english: Or are you ignorant of the bounties of God who is calling you to come yonder?

entries.farsi: ‏هر طرف كه بنگرى اعالم اوست / چون كريمى گويدت آتش در آ
entries.english: The whole world of six directions is His bounty: wherever you look, it is making Him known.

entries.farsi: اندر آ زود و مگو سوزد مرا / از انس فرزند مالك آمده ست
entries.english: When a generous man bids you come into the fire, come in quickly and do not say, “It will burn me.”

sections.title_fa: حكايت منديل در تنور پر آتش انداختن انس و ناسوختن
sections.title_en: The story of Anas, may God be pleased with him: how he cast a napkin into a fiery oven, and it was not burnt.

entries.farsi: ‏كه به مهمانى او شخصى شده ست / او حكايت كرد كز بعد طعام
entries.english: It has come concerning Anas son of Malik that a certain person became his guest.

entries.farsi: ‏ديد انس دستار خوان را زردفام / چركن و آلوده گفت اى خادمه
entries.english: He related that after the meal Anas saw the table-napkin yellow in hue,

entries.farsi: ‏اندر افكن در تنورش يك دمه / در تنور پر ز آتش در فكند
entries.english: Dirty and stained; and said, “O maid-servant, throw it into the oven at once.”

entries.farsi: آن زمان دستار خوان را هوشمند / جمله مهمانان در آن حيران شدند
entries.english: Thereupon the intelligent maid threw it into the oven, which was full of fire.

entries.farsi: انتظار دود كندورى بدند / بعد يك ساعت بر آورد از تنور
entries.english: All the guests were astounded thereat: they were in expectation of the smoke of the napkin.

entries.farsi: پاك و اسپيد و از آن اوساخ دور / قوم گفتند اى صحابى عزيز
entries.english: After a short time she took it out of the oven, clean and white and purged of that filth.

entries.farsi: چون نسوزيد و منقى گشت نيز / گفت ز انكه مصطفى دست و دهان
entries.english: The party said, “O venerable Companion, how didn’t it burn, and how too did it become cleansed?”

entries.farsi: ‏با چنان دست و لبى كن اقتراب / چون جمادى را چنين تشريف داد
entries.english: He replied, “Because Mustafa often rubbed his hands and lips on this napkin.”

entries.farsi: ‏بس بماليد اندر اين دستار خوان / اى دل ترسنده از نار و عذاب
entries.english: O heart afraid of the fire and torment, draw nigh unto such a hand and lip as that!

entries.farsi: جان عاشق را چها خواهد گشاد / مر كلوخ كعبه را چون قبله كرد
entries.english: Since it bestowed such honour upon a lifeless object, what things will it reveal to the soul of the lover!

entries.farsi: خاك مردان باش اى جان در نبرد / بعد از آن گفتند با آن خادمه
entries.english: Inasmuch as he made the clods of the Ka‘ba the qibla, do you, O soul, be the dust of holy men in war.

entries.farsi: ‏تو نگويى حال خود با اين همه / چون فگندى زود آن از گفت وى
entries.english: Afterwards they said to the maid-servant, “Will not you tell your own feelings about all this?

entries.farsi: گيرم او برده ست در اسرار پى / اين چنين دستار خوان قيمتى
entries.english: Why did you quickly cast it at his behest? I suppose he was acquainted with the secrets,

entries.farsi: ‏چون فگندى اندر آتش اى ستى / گفت دارم بر كريمان اعتماد
entries.english: Why did you, mistress, throw such a precious napkin into the fire?”

entries.farsi: نيستم ز اكرام ايشان نااميد / ميزرى چه بود اگر او گويدم
entries.english: She answered, “I have confidence in the generous: I do not despair of their bounty.

entries.farsi: ‏در رو اندر عين آتش بى‏ندم / اندر افتم از كمال اعتماد
entries.english: What of a piece of cloth? If he bid me go without regret into the very essence of the fire,

entries.farsi: از عباد اللهَّ دارم بس اميد / سر در اندازم نه اين دستار خوان
entries.english: I, from perfect confidence, will fall in: I have great hope of them that are devoted to God.

entries.farsi: ‏ز اعتماد هر كريم راز دان / اى برادر خود بر اين اكسير زن
entries.english: I will throw myself in, not this napkin, because of my confidence in every generous one who knows the mystery.”

entries.farsi: آن دلى باشد كه كم ز اشكم بود
entries.english: O brother, apply yourself to this elixir: the faith of a man must not be less than the faith of a woman.

entries.farsi: ‏كم نبايد صدق مرد از صدق زن / آن دل مردى كه از زن كم بود
entries.english: The heart of the man that is less than a woman is the heart that is less than the belly.

sections.title_fa: قصه‏ى فرياد رسيدن رسول عليه السلام كاروان عرب را كه از تشنگى و بى‏آبى درمانده بودند و دل ‏بر مرگ نهاده شتران و خلق زبان بيرون انداخته
sections.title_en: Story of the Prophet’s, on whom be peace, coming to the aid of a caravan of Arabs who had been brought to sore straits by thirst and lack of water and had set their minds on death:
the camels and the people had let their tongues drop out.

entries.farsi: اندر آن وادى گروهى از عرب / ‏خشك شد از قطع بارانش قرب
entries.english: In that wadi a company of Arabs: their water-skins had become dry from lack of rain:

entries.farsi: در ميان آن بيابان مانده / ‏كاروانى مرگ خود بر خوانده
entries.english: A caravan amidst the desert in sore distress— they had rehearsed their own death.

entries.farsi: ناگهانى آن مغيث هر دو كون / ‏مصطفى پيدا شد از ره بهر عون
entries.english: Suddenly he who succours both worlds, Mustafa, appeared on the way, for help’s sake.

entries.farsi: ديد آن جا كاروانى بس بزرگ / ‏بر تف ريگ و ره صعب و سترگ
entries.english: He saw there an exceeding great caravan on the scalding sand and a hard and terrible journey;

entries.farsi: اشترانشان را زبان آويخته / ‏خلق اندر ريگ هر سو ريخته
entries.english: The tongues of their camels hanging out, the people strown everywhere on the sand.

entries.farsi: رحمتش آمد گفت هين زوتر رويد / چند يارى سوى آن كثبان دويد
entries.english: He took pity and said, “Listen, go at once, some of your comrades, and run to yonder sand hills,

entries.farsi: كه سياهى بر شتر مشك آورد / سوى مير خود به زودى مى‏برد
entries.english: For a Negro on camelback will bring a water-skin, he is conveying with all speed to his master.

entries.farsi: آن شتربان سيه را با شتر / سوى من آريد با فرمان مر
entries.english: Bring to me that Negro camel-driver along with the camel by force, if need be.”

entries.farsi: سوى كثبان آمدند آن طالبان / بعد يك ساعت بديدند آن چنان
entries.english: Those seekers approached the sand hills: after a short while they saw it was even so:

entries.farsi: بنده‏اى مى‏شد سيه با اشترى / ‏راويه پر آب چون هديه برى
entries.english: A Negro slave was going with a camel, the water-skin filled with water, like one bearing a gift.

entries.farsi: پس بدو گفتند مى‏خواند ترا / ‏اين طرف فخر البشر خير الورى
entries.english: Then they said to him, “The Pride of mankind, the Best of created beings, invites you in this direction.”

entries.farsi: گفت من نشناسم او را كيست او / گفت او آن ماه روى قند خو
entries.english: He said, “I do not know him: who is he?” He said, “He is that moon-faced sweet-natured one.”

entries.farsi: نوعها تعريف كردندش كه هست / ‏گفت مانا او مگر آن شاعر است
entries.english: They described to him the diverse qualities which exist: he said, “Perhaps he is that poet

entries.farsi: كش كشانش آوريدند آن طرف / ‏او فغان برداشت در تشنيع و تف
entries.english: Who subdued a multitude by magic: I will not come half a span towards him.”

entries.farsi: كه گروهى را زبون كرد او به سحر / من نيايم جانب او نيم شبر
entries.english: Dragging him along, they brought him there: he raised an outcry in revilement and heat.

entries.farsi: جمله را ز آن مشك او سيراب كرد / اشتران و هر كسى ز آن آب خورد
entries.english: When they dragged him before that venerable one, he said, “Drink the water and in addition carry it away .”

entries.farsi: چون كشيدندش به پيش آن عزيز / گفت نوشيد آب و برداريد نيز
entries.english: He satisfied the thirst of them all from that water-skin: the camels and every person drank of that water.

entries.farsi: راويه پر كرد و مشك از مشك او / ابر گردون خيره ماند از رشك او
entries.english: From his water-skin he filled large and small water-skins: from jealousy of him the clouds in the sky were distraught.

entries.farsi: اين كسى ديده ست كز يك راويه / ‏سرد گردد سوز چندان هاويه
entries.english: Has anyone seen this, that the burning glow of so many Hells should be cooled by a single water-skin?

entries.farsi: اين كسى ديده ست كز يك مشك آب / ‏گشت چندين مشك پر بى‏اضطراب
entries.english: Has anyone seen this, that all these water-skins were filled from a single water-skin without trouble?

entries.farsi: مشك خود رو پوش بود و موج فضل / مى‏رسيد از امر او از بحر اصل
entries.english: The water-skin itself was a veil, and at his command the waves of bounty were coming from the Sea of origin.

entries.farsi: آب از جوشش همى‏گردد هوا / و آن هوا گردد ز سردى آبها
entries.english: “Water by boiling is converted into air, and that air by cold is turned into water.”

entries.farsi: بلكه بى‏اسباب و بيرون زين حكم / ‏آب رويانيد تكوين از عدم
entries.english: No; without cause and beyond these maxims of philosophy the act of bringing into existence produced the water from non-existence.

entries.farsi: تو ز طفلى چون سببها ديده‏اى / ‏در سبب از جهل بر چفسيده‏اى
entries.english: Inasmuch as you have observed causes from your childhood, through ignorance you have stuck to the cause.

entries.farsi: با سببها از مسبب غافلى / سوى اين رو پوشها ز آن مايلى
entries.english: With causes you are forgetful of the Causer: hence you are inclining towards these veils.

entries.farsi: چون سببها رفت بر سر مى‏زنى / ‏ربنا و ربناها مى‏كنى
entries.english: When causes are gone, you will beat your head and cry many a time, “O our Lord! O our Lord!”

entries.farsi: رب مى‏گويد برو سوى سبب / ‏چون ز صنعم ياد كردى اى عجب
entries.english: The Lord will say, “Take yourself to the cause! How have you remembered My work? Oh, wonderful!”

entries.farsi: گفت زين پس من ترا بينم همه / ‏ننگرم سوى سبب و آن دمدمه
entries.english: He says, “Henceforth I will behold You entirely: I will not look towards the cause and that deception.”

entries.farsi: گويدش رُدُّوا لَعادُوا كار تست / ‏اى تو اندر توبه و ميثاق سست
entries.english: He will reply to him, “Your case is, ‘they were sent back, they would surely return,’ O you who are weak in your repentance and covenant;

entries.farsi: ليك من آن ننگرم رحمت كنم / ‏رحمتم پر ست بر رحمت تنم
entries.english: But I will not regard that, I will show mercy: My mercy is abounding, I will be intent on mercy.

entries.farsi: ننگرم عهد بدت بدهم عطا / از كرم اين دم چو مى‏خوانى مرا
entries.english: I will not regard your bad promise; I from loving kindness will bestow the gift at this moment, since you art calling unto Me.”

entries.farsi: قافله حيران شد اندر كار او / يا محمد چيست اين اى بحر خو
entries.english: The caravan were amazed at his deed. “O Muhammad, O you that have the nature of the Sea, what is this?

entries.farsi: كرده‏اى رو پوش مشك خرد را / غرقه كردى هم عرب هم كرد را
entries.english: You have made a small water-skin a veil: you have drowned (satisfied the thirst of) both Arabs and Kurds.”

sections.title_fa: مشك آن غالم از غيب پر آب كردن به معجزه و آن غالم سياه را سپيد رو كردن باذن اللهَّ تعالى
sections.title_en: How he miraculously filled the slave’s water-skin with water from the Unseen World and made the face of that black slave white by permission of God most High.

entries.farsi: اى غالم اكنون تو پر بين مشك خود / تا نگويى در شكايت نيك و بد
entries.english: “O slave, now behold your water-skin full, that you may not say good or bad in complaint.”

entries.farsi: آن سيه حيران شد از برهان او / مى‏دميد از المكان ايمان او
entries.english: The Negro was astounded at his evidentiary miracle: his faith was dawning from non-spatiality.

entries.farsi: چشمه‏اى ديد از هوا ريزان شده / ‏مشك او رو پوش فيض آن شده
entries.english: He saw that a fountain had begun to pour from the air and that his water-skin had become a veil to the emanation of that.

entries.farsi: ز آن نظر رو پوشها هم بر دريد / تا معين چشمه‏ى غيبى بديد
entries.english: The veils also were rent by that sight, so that he distinctly beheld the fountain of the Unseen.

entries.farsi: چشمها پر آب كرد آن دم غالم / ‏شد فراموشش ز خواجه و ز مقام
entries.english: Thereupon the slave’s eyes were filled with tears: he forgot his master and his dwelling-place.

entries.farsi: باز بهر مصلحت بازش كشيد / كه به خويش آ باز رو اى مستفيد
entries.english: Strength failed him to go on his way: God cast a mighty commotion into his soul.

entries.farsi: دست و پايش ماند از رفتن به راه / ‏زلزله افكند در جانش اله
entries.english: Then again he drew him back for good, saying, “Come to yourself; return, O you who will gain advantage.

entries.farsi: وقت حيرت نيست حيرت پيش تست / ‏اين زمان در ره در آ چالاك و چست
entries.english: It is not the time for bewilderment: bewilderment is in front of you; just now advance on your way briskly and speedily.”

entries.farsi: دستهاى مصطفى بر رو نهاد / بوسه‏هاى عاشقانه بس بداد
entries.english: He laid the hands of Mustafa on his face and gave many loving kisses.

entries.farsi: شد سپيد آن زنگى و زاده‏ى حبش / همچو بدر و روز روشن شد شبش
entries.english: Then Mustafa rubbed his blessed hand on his face and made it fortunate.

entries.farsi: مصطفى دست مبارك بر رخش / ‏آن زمان ماليد و كرد او فرخش
entries.english: That Abyssinian became white as the full moon, and his night turned into bright day.

entries.farsi: او همى‏شد بى‏سر و بى‏پاى مست / ‏پاى مى‏نشناخت در رفتن ز دست
entries.english: He became a Joseph in beauty and in coquetry: he said to him, “Now go home and relate what has befallen you.”

entries.farsi: يوسفى شد در جمال و در دالل / ‏گفتش اكنون رو بده واگوى حال
entries.english: He was going along, without head or foot, intoxicated: in going he knew not foot from hand.

entries.farsi: پس بيامد با دو مشك پر روان / ‏سوى خواجه از نواحى كاروان
entries.english: Then from the neighbourhood of the caravan he came hastening with two full water-skins to his master.

sections.title_fa: ديدن خواجه غالم خود را سپيد و ناشناختن كه اوست و گفتن كه غالم مرا تو كشته‏اى ‏ خونت گرفت و خدا ترا به دست من انداخت
sections.title_en: How the master saw his slave white and did not recognise him and said, “You have killed my slave: the murder has found you out, and God has thrown you into my hands.”

entries.farsi: اين يكى بدرى است مى‏آيد ز دور / مى‏زند بر نور روز از روش نور
entries.english: The master espied him from afar and remained bewildered: from amazement he called the people of the village.

entries.farsi: خواجه از دورش بديد و خيره ماند / از تحير اهل آن ده را بخواند
entries.english: “This,” said he, “is my water-skin and my camel: where, then, is my dark browed slave gone?

entries.farsi: راويه‏ى ما اشتر ما هست اين / ‏پس كجا شد بنده‏ى زنگى جبين
entries.english: This man coming from afar is a full-moon: the light from his countenance strikes upon the daylight.

entries.farsi: كو غالم ما مگر سر گشته شد / يا بدو گرگى رسيد و كشته شد
entries.english: Where is my slave? Perchance he has lost his way, or a wolf has overtaken him and he has been killed.”

entries.farsi: چون بيامد پيش گفتش كيستى / ‏از يمن زادى و يا تركيستى
entries.english: When he came before him, he said, “Who are you? Are you a native of Yemen or a Turcoman?

entries.farsi: گفت اگر كشتم به تو چون آمدم / ‏چون به پاى خود در اين خون آمدم
entries.english: Tell, what have you done to my slave? Speak the truth! If you have killed him, declare it! Do not seek evasion.”

entries.farsi: گو غالمم را چه كردى راست گو / گر بكشتى وانما حيلت مجو
entries.english: He replied, “If I have killed him, how have I come to you? How have I come with my own feet into this blood?”

entries.farsi: كو غالم من بگفت اينك منم / ‏كرد دست فضل يزدان روشنم
entries.english: “Where is my slave?” He said, “Lo, I am: the hand of God’s grace has made me resplendent.”

entries.farsi: هى چه مى‏گويى غالم من كجاست / ‏هين نخواهى رست از من جز به راست
entries.english: “Eh, what are you saying? Where is my slave? Listen, you will not escape from me except by the truth.”

entries.farsi: گفت اسرار ترا با آن غالم / ‏جمله واگويم يكايك من تمام
entries.english: He said, “I will relate all your secret dealings with that slave, one by one;

entries.farsi: ز آن زمانى كه خريدى تو مرا / تا به اكنون باز گويم ماجرا
entries.english: I will relate what has passed from the time when you did purchase me until now,

entries.farsi: تا بدانى كه همانم در وجود / گر چه از شبديز من صبحى گشود
entries.english: That you may know I am the same in existence, though a dawn has opened forth from my night-hued.

entries.farsi: رنگ ديگر شد و ليكن جان پاك / ‏فارغ از رنگ است و از اركان و خاك
entries.english: The colour is changed; but the pure spirit is free from colour and from the elements and the dust.”

entries.farsi: تن شناسان زود ما را گم كنند / آب نوشان ترك مشك و خم كنند
entries.english: They that know the body soon lose us; they that quaff the water abandon the water-skin and jar.

entries.farsi: جان شناسان از عددها فارغند / غرقه‏ى درياى بى‏چونند و چند
entries.english: They that know the spirit are free from numbers: they are sunk in the Sea that is without quality or quantity.

entries.farsi: جان شو و از راه جان جان را شناس / ‏يار بينش شو نه فرزند قياس
entries.english: Become spirit and know spirit by means of spirit: become the friend of vision, not the child of logic.

entries.farsi: چون ملك با عقل يك سر رشته‏اند / بهر حكمت را دو صورت گشته‏اند
entries.english: Forasmuch as the Angel is one in origin with Intelligence, they have become two forms for the sake of Wisdom—

entries.farsi: آن ملك چون مرغ بال و پر گرفت / ‏وين خرد بگذاشت پر و فر گرفت
entries.english: The Angel assumed wings and pinions like a bird while this Intelligence left wings and assumed splendour—

entries.farsi: الجرم هر دو مناصر آمدند / هر دو خوش رو پشت همديگر شدند
entries.english: Necessarily both became helpers: both the beauteous ones became a support to one another.

entries.farsi: هم ملك هم عقل حق را واجدى / ‏هر دو آدم را معين و ساجدى
entries.english: The Angel as well as the Intelligence is a finder of God: each of the two is a helper and worshipper of Adam.

entries.farsi: آن كه آدم را بدن ديد او رميد / و انكه نور موتمن ديد او خميد
entries.english: The nafs and the Devil have been one from the first, and have been an enemy and envier of Adam.

entries.farsi: نفس و شيطان بوده ز اول واحدى / ‏بوده آدم را عدو و حاسدى
entries.english: He that regarded Adam as a body fled, while he that regarded the trusty Light bowed.

entries.farsi: آن دو ديده روشنان بودند از اين / ‏وين دو را ديده نديده غير طين
entries.english: Those two were clairvoyant by this, while the eye of these two saw nothing but clay.

entries.farsi: اين بيان اكنون چو خر بر يخ بماند / چون نشايد بر جهود انجيل خواند
entries.english: This discourse is now left like an ass on the ice, since it is not fitting to recite the Gospel to Jews.

entries.farsi: كى توان با شيعه گفتن از عمر / كى توان بربط زدن در پيش كر
entries.english: How can one speak of ‘Umar to Shiites? How can one play a lute before the deaf?

entries.farsi: ليك گر در ده به گوشه يك كس است / ‏هاى و هويى كه بر آوردم بس است
entries.english: But if there is any one in a nook in the village, the hue and cry that I have raised is enough.

entries.farsi: مستحق شرح را سنگ و كلوخ / ناطقى گردد مشرح با رسوخ
entries.english: To him that is worthy to the exposition, stones and brickbats become an articulate and well-grounded exponent.

sections.title_fa: بيان آن كه حق تعالى هر چه داد و آفريد از سماوات و ارضين و اعيان و اعراض همه به استدعاى حاجت آفريد، خود را محتاج چيزى بايد كردن تا بدهد كه أَ مَّنْ يُجِيبُ الْمُضْ طَرَّ إِذا دَعاهُ، اضطرار گواه استحقاق است

sections.title_en: Explaining that whatever God most High bestowed and created— the heavens, the earth, the substances and the forms— He created all at the demand of need, and that one must make one’s self in need of a thing, so that He may bestow it; for “… Or He who answers the sorely distressed when he calls unto Him?”
Sore distress is the evidence of worthiness.

entries.farsi: آن نياز مريمى بوده ست و درد / كه چنان طفلى سخن آغاز كرد
entries.english: It was Mary’s want and pain that made such a babe begin to speak.

entries.farsi: جزو او بى‏او براى او بگفت / ‏جزو جزوت گفت دارد در نهفت
entries.english: Part of her spoke on her behalf without her: every part of you has speech in secret.

entries.farsi: دست و پا شاهد شوندت اى رهى / ‏منكرى را چند دست و پا نهى
entries.english: Your hands and feet become witnesses, O slave: how long will you set hand and foot to denial?

entries.farsi: ور نباشى مستحق شرح و گفت / ‏ناطقه‏ى ناطق ترا ديد و بخفت
entries.english: And if you are not worthy of the exposition and the speech, the rational soul of the speaker saw you and went to sleep.

entries.farsi: هر چه روييد از پى محتاج رست / نابيابد طالبى چيزى كه جست
entries.english: Whatever grew has grown for the sake of the needy, in order that a seeker may find the thing he sought.

entries.farsi: حق تعالى گر سماوات آفريد / از براى دفع حاجات آفريد
entries.english: If God most High has created the heavens, He has created them for the purpose of removing needs.

entries.farsi: هر كجا دردى دوا آن جا رود / هر كجا فقرى نوا آن جا رود
entries.english: Wherever a pain is, the cure goes there; wherever poverty is, the provision goes there.

entries.farsi: هر كجا مشكل جواب آن جا رود / هر كجا كشتى است آب آن جا رود
entries.english: Wherever a difficult question is, the answer goes there; wherever a ship is, the water goes there.

entries.farsi: آب كم جو تشنگى آور به دست / ‏تا بجوشد آبت از بالا و پست
entries.english: Do not seek the water, get thirst, so that the water may gush forth from above and below.

entries.farsi: تا نزايد طفلك نازك گلو / كى روان گردد ز پستان شير او
entries.english: Until the tender-throated babe is born, how should the milk for it begin to flow from the breast?

entries.farsi: رو بدين بالا و پستيها بدو / تا شوى تشنه و حرارت را گرو
entries.english: Go; run on these hills and dales, to the end that you may become thirsty and a prey to heat;

entries.farsi: بعد از آن از بانگ زنبور هوا / بانگ آب جو بنوشى اى كيا
entries.english: After that, from the noise of the hornet of the air you will hear the noise of the water of the stream, O king.

entries.farsi: حاجت تو كم نباشد از حشيش / ‏آب را گيرى سوى او مى‏كشيش
entries.english: Your need is not less than dry plants: you take water and are drawing it towards them;

entries.farsi: گوش گيرى آب را تو مى‏كشى / ‏سوى زرع خشك تا يابد خوشى
entries.english: You take the water by the ear and draw it towards the dry crops that they may obtain refreshment.

entries.farsi: زرع جان را كش جواهر مضمر است / ‏ابر رحمت پر ز آب كوثر است
entries.english: For the spiritual crops, whose essences are concealed, the cloud of mercy is full of the water of Kawthar.

entries.farsi: تا سَقاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ آيد خطاب / ‏تشنه باش اللهَّ أعلم بالصواب
entries.english: In order that their Lord gave them to drink may be addressed, be thirsty! God best knows the right course.

sections.title_fa: آمدن آن زن كافر با طفل شير خواره به نزديك مصطفى عليه السلام و ناطق شدن ‏ عيسى‏وار به معجزات رسول صلى اللهَّ عليه و آله
sections.title_en: How the unbelieving woman came to Mustafa, on whom be peace, with a sucking babe,
and how it spoke, like Jesus, of the miracles of the Prophet, God bless and save him!

entries.farsi: هم از آن ده يك زنى از كافران / ‏سوى پيغمبر دوان شد ز امتحان
entries.english: A woman of the same village, one of the unbelievers, ran to the Prophet for the sake of testing.

entries.farsi: پيش پيغمبر در آمد با خمار / كودكى دو ماهه زن را بر كنار
entries.english: She came in to the Prophet, with the veil: the woman had a two months old infant in her lap.

entries.farsi: گفت كودك سلم اللهَّ عليك / ‏يا رسول اللهَّ قد جئنا إليك
entries.english: The child said, “God give peace unto you, O Messenger of Allah! We have come to you.”

entries.farsi: مادرش از خشم گفتش هى خموش / ‏كيت افكند اين شهادت را بگوش
entries.english: Its mother said to it angrily, “Hey, be silent! Who put this testimony into yours ear?

entries.farsi: اين كى‏ات آموخت اى طفل صغير / كه زبانت گشت در طفلى جرير
entries.english: Who taught you this, O little child, so that your tongue became fluent in infancy?”

entries.farsi: گفت حق آموخت آن گه جبرئيل / ‏در بيان با جبرئيل من رسيل
entries.english: It replied, “God taught, then Gabriel: I am Gabriel’s accompanist in declaration.”

entries.farsi: گفت كو گفتا كه بالاى سرت / ‏مى‏نبينى كن به بالا منظرت
entries.english: She said, “Where?” It replied, “Above your head: do not you see? Turn yours eye aloft.

entries.farsi: ايستاده بر سر تو جبرئيل / ‏مر مرا گشته به صد گونه دليل
entries.english: Gabriel is standing above you: to me he has become a guide in a hundred diverse ways.”

entries.farsi: گفت مى‏بينى تو گفتا كه بلى / ‏بر سرت تابان چو بدرى كاملى
entries.english: She said, “Do you see?” “Yes,” it replied; “shining above you like a perfect full-moon.

entries.farsi: مى‏بياموزد مرا وصف رسول / ‏ز آن علوم مى‏رهاند زين سفول
entries.english: He is teaching me the qualities of the Prophet and delivering me by means of that sublimity from this degradation.”

entries.farsi: پس رسولش گفت اى طفل رضيع / ‏چيست نامت باز گو و شو مطيع
entries.english: Then said the Prophet to it, “O sucking child, what is your name? Say forth and comply.”

entries.farsi: گفت نامم پيش حق عبد العزيز / عبد عزى پيش اين يك مشت هيز
entries.english: “‘Abdu ’l-‘Aziz,” said the child, “is my name with God; Abd-i Uzza with this handful of reprobates.

entries.farsi: من ز عزى پاك و بيزار و برى / ‏حق آن كه دادت اين پيغمبرى
entries.english: I am clear and free and quit of Uzza, by the truth of Him who gave you this prophethood.”

entries.farsi: كودك دو ماهه همچون ماه بدر / درس بالغ گفته چون اصحاب صدر
entries.english: The two months old child, like the full-moon, pronounced the discourse of an adult, as those who occupy the seat of honour.

entries.farsi: هر دو مى‏گفتند كز خوف سقوط / جان سپردن به بر اين بوى حنوط
entries.english: Then at that instant arrived balm from Paradise, so that the brain of child and mother drew in the scent.

entries.farsi: پس حنوط آن دم ز جنت در رسيد / تا دماغ طفل و مادر بو كشيد
entries.english: Both were saying, “For fear of falling it is best to surrender one’s soul to the scent of this balm.”

entries.farsi: آن كسى را كش خدا حافظ بود / مرغ و ماهى مر و را حارس شود
entries.english: As for that one whom God endows with knowledge, things inanimate and growing utter a hundred expressions of belief in him.

entries.farsi: آن كسى را كش معرف حق بود / جامد و ناميش صد صدق زند
entries.english: As for that one whom God protects, birds and fish become his guardians.

sections.title_fa: ربودن عقاب موزه‏ى مصطفى عليه الصالة و السلام و بردن بر هوا و نگون ‏ كردن و از موزه مار سياه فرو افتادن
sections.title_en: How an eagle seized the boot of the Prophet, on whom be peace, and carried it into the air and turned it upside down, and how a black serpent dropped down from the boot.

entries.farsi: اندر اين بودند كاواز صال / مصطفى بشنيد از سوى عال
entries.english: They were thus engaged, when Mustafa heard from aloft the call to the ritual prayer.

entries.farsi: خواست آبى و وضو را تازه كرد / دست و رو را شست او ز آن آب سرد
entries.english: He asked for water and renewed the ablution: he washed his hands and face with that cold water.

entries.farsi: هر دو پا شست و به موزه‏كرد راى / ‏موزه را بربود يك موزه رباى
entries.english: He washed both his feet and gave attention to his boot: a boot-snatcher carried off the boot.

entries.farsi: دست سوى موزه برد آن خوش خطاب / ‏موزه را بربود از دستش عقاب
entries.english: That man of sweet address moved his hand towards the boot: an eagle snatched the boot from his hand,

entries.farsi: موزه را اندر هوا برد او چو باد / پس نگون كرد و از آن مارى فتاد
entries.english: And bore it away into the air, as the wind; then she turned it upside down, and a serpent dropped from it.

entries.farsi: در فتاد از موزه يك مار سياه / ‏ز آن عنايت شد عقابش نيك خواه
entries.english: From the boot dropped a black serpent: on account of that care the eagle became his benevolent friend.

entries.farsi: پس عقاب آن موزه را آورد باز / گفت هين بستان و رو سوى نماز
entries.english: Then the eagle brought back the boot and said, “Come, take it and go to prayers.

entries.farsi: از ضرورت كردم اين گستاخيى / ‏من ز ادب دارم شكسته شاخيى
entries.english: I did this presumptuous act from necessity: I am abashed by reverence.

entries.farsi: واى كاو گستاخ پايى مى‏نهد / بى‏ضرورت كش هوا فتوى دهد
entries.english: Woe to him that steps presumptuously without necessity because vain desire authorizes him!”

entries.farsi: پس رسولش شكر كرد و گفت ما / اين جفا ديديم و بود اين خود وفا
entries.english: Then the Prophet thanked her and said, “I deemed this rudeness, but it really was kindness.

entries.farsi: موزه بربودى و من درهم شدم / ‏تو غمم بردى و من در غم شدم
entries.english: You carried off the boot, and I was perturbed: you took away my grief, and I was aggrieved.

entries.farsi: گر چه هر غيبى خدا ما را نمود / دل در آن لحظه به خود مشغول بود
entries.english: Although God has shown to me every unseen thing, at that moment my heart was occupied with myself.”

entries.farsi: گفت دور از تو كه غفلت در تو رست / ‏ديدنم آن غيب را هم عكس تست
entries.english: She (the eagle) said, “Far be it from you that forgetfulness grew up in you: my seeing that invisible thing is your reflection.

entries.farsi: مار در موزه ببينم بر هوا / ‏نيست از من عكس تست اى مصطفى
entries.english: I, in the air, see the serpent in the boot, it is not of me, and it is your reflection, O Mustafa.”

entries.farsi: عكس نورانى همه روشن بود / عكس ظلمانى همه گلخن بود
entries.english: The reflection of the man of light is wholly resplendent; the reflection of the man of darkness is wholly a bath-stove ash-heap.

entries.farsi: عكس عبد اهلل همه نورى بود / عكس بيگانه همه كورى بود
entries.english: The reflection of the servant of God is wholly luminous; the reflection of the stranger is wholly blindness.
